type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment:
Emissions of carbon monoxide from the ammonia plant are a result of combustion of natural gas as well as infrequent
venting of process gas from the ammonia plant during abnormal operating conditions. Starting up and shutting down the ammonia plant will require venting of significant quantities of process gas containing carbon monoxide at various points of the process. The quantity vented each year can vary significantly depending on how many times the plant had to be shut down. Plant shut downs may be due to scheduled turnarounds or unplanned process upsets or equipment failure. There were no planned or unplanned ammonia plant startup/shutdown venting events in 2015. Reported carbon monoxide emissions are exclusively from natural gas combustion.  In 2014, there was an extended scheduled outage that vented 133 tonnes of carbon monoxide, in addition to three unscheduled outages.
No catalyst disposal in 2015.  Pond remediation activities resulted in higher underground injection disposal well volumes.
No copper bearing catalyst was recycled in 2015.
No nickel bearing catalyst was disposed in 2015.  Pond remediation activities resulted in higher underground injection disposal well volumes.
No nickel bearing catalyst was recycled in 2015.
Pond remediation activities resulted in higher underground injection disposal well volumes.
No zinc bearing catalyst recycled in 2015.
Typical year to year variations in quantities in wastewater.

type.fr: Commentaire sur les rejets sur le site

comments.comment: Emissions of carbon monoxide from the ammonia plant are a result of combustion of natural gas as well as infrequent venting of process gas from the ammonia plant during abnormal operating conditions. Starting up and shutting down the ammonia plant will require venting of significant quantities of process gas containing carbon monoxide at various points of the process.  The quantity vented each year can vary significantly depending on how many times the plant had to be shut down. Plant shut downs may be due to scheduled turnarounds or unplanned process upsets or equipment failure. There were three ammonia plant startup/shutdown venting events in 2016, one planned and two unplanned, that vented quantities of carbon monoxide in process gas.  There were no ammonia plant startup/shutdown venting events in 2015, and as such the lower than usual carbon monoxide emissions were exclusively due to natural gas combustion.
